content.full_text:
RAVI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION

August 18, 1995


Robert J. Smith
7220 Greenhaven Dr, Ste. 1
Sacramento, CA 95831


Re: Your Request For Confirmation
of Telephone Advice
Our File No. I-95-268


Dear Mr. Smith:

You have requested confirmation of advice given to you by
telephone regarding the campaign disclosure provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 Since you are requesting
general advice, we are treating your request as one for informal
assistance.2

In your letter you ask if the activity of "networking" for a
particular candidate or issue will result in the creation of a
committee which would be subject to the reporting requirements of
the Act. You indicate that you and your associates intend to call
friends or other associates to discuss the support or opposition
of candidates or issues. A part of that discussion may include
encouraging people to make contributions of time or money to
particular candidates or causes. You have also indicated that in

1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Government Code Section 83114; 2 Cal. Code of Regs. Sections
18329(c)(3).)


428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814 (916) 322-5660

Smith, Robert J.
Our File No. I-95-268
Page 2


all cases the people contacted through this telephone network will
make their checks payable directly to the candidates or committees
being supported. In some cases the checks will be sent directly
to the candidates or committees, but in other cases checks may be
collected by one of the networking associates to be forwarded on
to the recipient.

Your letter correctly states my advice that the activities
indicated in your letter will not result in the creation of a
committee as defined in the Act and will not result in any filing
obligations under the Act.

If you have additional questions, please contact me at (916)
322-5662.

Sincerely,

[signature]

Wayne P. Imberi
Political Reform Consultant

sections.question: If the activity of "networking" for a particular candidate or issue will result in the creation of a committee which would be subject to the reporting requirements of the Act. The questioner indicates that they and their associates intend to call friends or other associates to discuss the support or opposition of candidates or issues, and may encourage people to make contributions of time or money to particular candidates or causes. In some cases checks will be sent directly to the candidates or committees, but in other cases checks may be collected by one of the networking associates to be forwarded on to the recipient.
sections.conclusion: Your letter correctly states my advice that the activities indicated in your letter will not result in the creation of a committee as defined in the Act and will not result in any filing obligations under the Act.
sections.question_synthetic: Does informal "networking" activity involving telephone calls to discuss and encourage support for candidates or issues, where contributors send checks either directly or via an intermediary to those candidates or committees, trigger committee formation and reporting requirements under the Political Reform Act?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC advises that the described networking activities will not trigger the creation of a committee or any filing obligations under the Political Reform Act.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is a confirmation of prior telephone advice. No formal Q/A sections with headers, but question and conclusion are clearly identifiable in narrative text. Marked as informal assistance, which does not provide immunity. Letter reference number is 1-95-268.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: If the activity of "networking" for a particular candidate or issue will result in the creation of a committee which would be subject to the reporting requirements of the Act. The questioner indicates that they and their associates intend to call friends or other associates to discuss the support or opposition of candidates or issues, and may encourage people to make contributions of time or money to particular candidates or causes. In some cases checks will be sent directly to the candidates or committees, but in other cases checks may be collected by one of the networking associates to be forwarded on to the recipient.

CONCLUSION: Your letter correctly states my advice that the activities indicated in your letter will not result in the creation of a committee as defined in the Act and will not result in any filing obligations under the Act.

embedding.summary: This is a confirmation letter from the FPPC regarding telephone advice given to Robert J. Smith about whether informal networking activities designed to encourage contributions to candidates and issues would trigger committee formation requirements. The FPPC confirms that such networking activities do not create a committee or filing obligations under the Act.
